title: Merovingian script — a historical introduction
subtitle: The Frankish book-script that Carolingian reform replaced
excerpt: Merovingian is the 6th- to 8th-century Frankish script that evolved from late Roman cursive. It is one of the most difficult medieval hands for modern readers: compressed, heavily abbreviated, and full of ligatures that bear no resemblance to their component letters. Understanding where Merovingian sits in the Latin script timeline, and how to decode its most problematic letterforms, makes it accessible as both a reading subject and a historical context for calligraphers working through the full arc of Western script.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906): public domain, Internet Archive. Johnston's historical introduction covers the Latin script timeline from Roman capitals through uncial to the national hands. E.A. Lowe, Codices Latini Antiquiores (1934 onwards): scholarly palaeographical catalogue; older volumes are public domain. General description of Merovingian letterforms is synthesised from standard palaeographical sources; no direct quotation from copyright works.

glossaryTerms.definition: The national book-script of the Frankish kingdom, in use from roughly the 6th to 8th century. Evolved from late Roman cursive; characterised by heavy ligatures, contracted letterforms, and variable letter heights that make it difficult to read without practice.

The reformed Latin script developed under Charlemagne's scriptoria from roughly 800 onwards, replacing the various national scripts (including Merovingian) across the Frankish empire. Clear, well-separated letterforms with consistent height; the ancestor of modern lower-case letters.

One of the regional Latin scripts that developed independently in different parts of post-Roman Europe, including Merovingian (Frankish), Visigothic (Spanish), Beneventan (southern Italian), and Insular (Irish and English). Each adapted Roman letter-shapes to local writing habits.

Two or more letters written as a single connected stroke, the letterforms merged or abbreviated in the joining. Merovingian scribes used ligatures far more freely than later scripts; the ae, ct, and ti ligatures are among the most frequently encountered.

content.text: Merovingian script was the book-hand of the Frankish kingdom from about the 6th to the 8th century. Charlemagne's scholars reformed the Latin script around 800. They replaced the tangle of regional hands with the clear
Carolingian minuscule
. Merovingian was the script they were replacing. Understanding it shows why the reform took the shape it did. It gives useful context for anyone studying the history of Western calligraphy.
Where Merovingian fits in the Latin script timeline

The Latin script does not follow a single straight line from Roman capitals to the modern alphabet. When Roman authority withdrew from western Europe in the 5th century, Latin writing continued in local centres: monasteries, royal courts, and scriptoria. Without a centralising influence, scripts began to diverge. The result was a family of
national hands
: scripts that evolved independently from shared Roman roots. Merovingian is the Frankish branch of this family.

Its immediate ancestors are late Roman cursive minuscule (the everyday writing hand of the Roman empire), plus some influence from uncial and half-uncial. These were the more formal book-scripts that persisted into the early medieval period. Merovingian scribes valued speed and abbreviation over legibility to outsiders. The hand was designed to be read by those trained in it, not by general Latin readers. This context explains many of its unusual features.
Key features and difficult letterforms

The first thing a modern reader notices is the irregular height. In Carolingian and most post-medieval scripts, letters sit consistently on the baseline, with ascenders and descenders in predictable zones. In Merovingian script, letters vary noticeably in height even within a single word. Tall versions of the same letter appear alongside compressed ones, often because the scribe was adjusting to the line or compressing an abbreviation.

The Merovingian e is one of the most disorienting letters for modern readers. It is written as a high loop: a curve that rises well above the x-height and closes at the top without the open horizontal bar that defines a roman e. On first encounter it looks more like a Greek or Cyrillic character than a Latin letter. Once identified, it becomes the single most reliable landmark in reading Merovingian text.

The a in Merovingian is often written as a two-stroke cursive form that resembles a cc pair, quite unlike the open single-bowl a of Carolingian. In some manuscripts the a merges with adjacent letters through a connecting stroke below the baseline. The r is very compressed. What looks like an m or a ni sequence may resolve as ur once the r's characteristic descending hook is recognised.
Ligatures present the highest decoding challenge. Merovingian scribes used
ligatures

far more freely than later medieval hands. The ae ligature appears frequently and may look like a single angular stroke. The ti ligature creates a compound form that newer readers consistently misread as an f or a tall i. The ct ligature joins the two letters in a crossing hairline that can be invisible at small scale. Building a mental catalogue of the most common ligatures is the single most effective strategy for improving reading speed.
How to approach a Merovingian manuscript

The practical strategy for reading Merovingian is to spend time with a single manuscript before attempting to read it, rather than jumping straight into a transcription attempt. Work through these stages:

First, identify the e in as many words as possible. The high-loop e is distinctive enough to stand out from the surrounding letterforms. Once you can spot the e reliably, use it as an anchor: any word containing a known e can be partially decoded, and partial decoding often gives enough context to resolve the remaining letters.

Second, find the abbreviation marks. Merovingian manuscripts use conventional abbreviations throughout. A horizontal stroke over a vowel means a following m or n. The tironian et (an ampersand-like symbol) stands for et (and). Certain letter combinations are shortened to a single stroke with a superscript mark. Learning the most common Latin abbreviations from any source reduces the decoding load considerably.

Third, use the Latin text itself as a guide. If the document is a charter, a liturgical text, or a saint's life, large sections will follow conventional formulae. The opening phrase of a charter (which grants or confirms a gift), the closing witness list, and the dating formula all follow recognisable patterns. Work from the formula outward.
Merovingian and the national-hand family

Merovingian is one member of a family of national hands. The family includes Visigothic (the Spanish script), Beneventan (the hand of Monte Cassino and southern Italy), and the Insular hands (Irish Half-uncial and Anglo-Saxon minuscule). Each developed from late-Roman cursive roots. Each arrived at different solutions to the same problems. A calligrapher who can read one national hand finds the others less foreign than expected: the shared ancestry shows.

The Carolingian reform did not end Merovingian overnight. In some Frankish monasteries the older hand persisted for a generation or more. Carolingian served the main text; Merovingian appeared in annotations and local documents. Manuscripts from this period show both scripts on the same page.
Modern attempts to revive
Merovingian script

as a practised calligraphic hand are rare. The letterforms are hard to standardise, nib-angle conventions are not fully documented, and the visual result is opaque to most viewers. The hand is most valuable as a reading and historical subject rather than a writing subject. A calligrapher who studies it and then returns to Foundational or Italic will find their sense of the tradition considerably deepened.
